notes: Model baselines. Every figure carries {value, source, url, retrieved, status}. status: verified = checked against the named public source at build time; reported = public reporting on the live 2026 crisis; estimate = derived or approximate, marked as such in the UI; brief-baseline = from the Analytica build brief pending a fresh primary-source check. Rebuild with scripts/fetch.mjs.

hormuz_throughput_mbd.source: EIA — World Oil Transit Chokepoints (H1 2025 average)
hormuz_throughput_mbd.note: Pre-crisis baseline. Roughly 20% of global petroleum liquids consumption and ~25–30% of seaborne crude.

hormuz_share_seaborne_crude.source: EIA WOTC — ~25–30% of seaborne crude (H1 2025 update commentary)

lng_share_global.note: Essentially all Qatari (plus UAE volumes). No overland bypass exists for LNG.

global_liquids_mbd.source: EIA — Short-Term Energy Outlook, world petroleum and other liquids supply (2026 projections, pre-disruption basis; 2026 STEOs project ~104–106 M b/d)
global_liquids_mbd.note: Denominator uses the pre-disruption supply basis — the model asks what share of a normal world the stranded barrels are.

spr_releasable_mbbl.source: IEA — emergency response; government-controlled public stocks of IEA members (~1.2 bn bbl). US SPR ~400 M bbl of that (DOE).
spr_releasable_mbbl.note: Order-of-magnitude runway figure. Actual drawdown rates are capped well below the stranded rate; the runway is a stock/flow ratio, not a release plan.

current_reported_disruption.source: 2026 crisis reporting — non-Iranian transits down ~90% (108 → 25/week, inbound to 8 vessels) after Iran re-declared closure 12 Jul and the US blockade was reinstated

opec_spare_mbd.unit: million barrels/day OPEC+ surplus crude production capacity
opec_spare_mbd.source: EIA STEO — OPEC surplus production capacity. ~90% of it is Saudi and Emirati — physically inside the Gulf.
opec_spare_mbd.note: The classic fallacy: spare capacity that must transit the strait it is meant to offset counts for nothing during a closure — only bypass-connected spare does.

max_release_mbd.unit: million barrels/day, maximum feasible coordinated release rate
max_release_mbd.source: 2022 IEA coordinated actions — 240 M bbl over ~6 months; US SPR drawdown peaked near 1 M b/d. The binding constraint on reserves is drawdown rate, not stock size.

eps_longrun.unit: long-run oil demand elasticity (≈1–2 years: substitution + demand destruction)
eps_longrun.source: Literature meta-analyses of oil demand elasticity; IMF WEO analytical chapters

tau_months.unit: months — adjustment time constant for elasticity ramp ε(t)

cpi_per_10pct.unit: pp added to CPI per +10% oil price, one-year horizon
cpi_per_10pct.source: IMF WEO analyses; Federal Reserve staff rules of thumb

gdp_per_10pct.unit: pp global GDP drag per +10% oil price, sustained
gdp_per_10pct.source: IMF WEO analyses; OECD interlink rules of thumb

eps_short_run.source: Literature range for short-run oil demand elasticity; see Methodology

petroline.name: Petroline (East–West)
petroline.source: EIA WOTC; Aramco expansion reporting. Crisis observation: Yanbu loadings averaging ~3.75 M b/d (Lloyd's List Intelligence, Jul 2026) — activated spare ≈1.75 M b/d, slightly ABOVE the EIA-conservative 1.5 allocation.

adcop.name: Habshan–Fujairah (ADCOP)
adcop.source: EIA WOTC; ADNOC disclosures. Routine use limits excess capacity.

eia.label: Conservative — EIA spare bypass
eia.source: EIA WOTC — ~2.6 M b/d of unused bypass capacity. Per-pipeline allocation is Analytica's derived estimate summing to the EIA total.

expanded.label: Expanded — post-2025 Petroline expansion
expanded.source: Upper bound of public commentary (3.5–5.5 M b/d) after Aramco's claimed 7 M b/d Petroline capacity; sustained flows at that level are untested.
